utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so beverly is a 53 -year-old female with a recent diagnosis of stage three nonsmile cell lung cancer who presents for follow-up during neo agit chemotherapy she was diagnosed with a four . four centimeter left upper lobe nodule biopsy was positive for adenocarcinoma molecular testing is pending at this time alright hello beverly how are you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm good today

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're good today yeah you've been going through a lot lately i know you just had your treatment how how are your symptoms

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: my symptoms are pretty good today i just kind of have a minimal cough and a sore throat

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: but that's all i'm feeling today

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and how about fatigue have you been feeling more tired

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes a little bit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and how about any nausea or vomiting

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no not as of today

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and i know you were mentioning a cough before how is it as far as walking are you having any shortness of breath

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i have n't noticed any shortness of breath it just kind of seems to be a lingering kind of light dry cough

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: cough okay is it any mucus with it or is it a dry cough

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: more dry

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: a dry cough okay and tell me more about this sore throat

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: this kind of seems to be persistent comes and goes it will be worse sometimes and then others it feels better trying to drink lots of fluids

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: to see if it can it you know the dry coughing if it's part of that or what i can do

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and when you mention drinking and eating is do you feel like anything is getting stuck there

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i do n't feel like anything is getting stuck right now and i have n't been i have been eating but not as much as i normally would

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay alright and how are you doing as far as like just emotionally and mentally how are you doing i'm just talking a little bit about your support systems

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: the nursing staff and the office has been very good to help you know with anything that i need as far as support so just since we are just getting started so far on the journey i do feel like i have support and mentally you know still feel strong

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and how about with family or friends have you been able to turn to anyone

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do have good family members that have been supportive and they have come to my treatment with me

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay excellent excellent and so right now you're on a combination of two different chemotherapies the cisplestan as well as the eupside and you had your last treatment just a few days ago but you're saying right now you've been able to tolerate the nausea and the fatigue

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes i have n't had any nausea but you know just slight fatigue it does n't seem to be overwhelming

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay so we are gon na go ahead if it's okay with you and start your physical exam reviewing your vitals so vitals look good especially your oxygen especially with the chemotherapy you've been getting and the cough so your oxygen looks good so i'm happy with that so now i'm just examining your neck especially with your sore throat and i do n't appreciate any cervical lymphadenopathy and also no supraclavicular adenopathy listening to your heart you have a nice regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs that i appreciate now on your lung exam when you're taking some deep breaths i do notice some crackles in your lungs bilaterally and what that means is there is there is some faint sounds that i'm hearing which could represent some fluid there so on looking at your skin exam on your chest you do have some erythema on the anterior side of the chest on the left side and this could be related to the radiation so on your lower extremities i appreciate no edema and everything else looks good and thank you i know you did a chest x-ray before coming in so on your results for the chest x-ray it does look like you have some mild radiation pneumonitis which basically means some inflammation of the lungs most likely due to the radiation so what does this all mean so for your assessment and plan so for the first diagnosis the first problem of the lung cancer so what we're gon na do is we're gon na continue with the current regimen of your chemotherapy of the cisplacin and the etoside and we're gon na continue with your current dose of radiation at forty five grade and when that's complete we will repeat some imaging and hopefully you know the tumor will shrink down enough that we can remove it surgically okay for problem number two so the radiation pneumonitis so that's what causing that cough as well as some of the shortness of breath i know you're not experiencing it much now so what i'm gon na do for that is actually gon na prescribe you a low dose of prednisone and so that's an will help with the inflammation i'm gon na give you forty milligrams daily for five days and so hopefully that will help reduce the inflammation and so that you can continue with the radiation okay how does that sound so far

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that sounds great thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then lastly for the painful swallowing that you're having so the inflammation you're having it not only in your lungs but it also in your esophagus as well so what i'm gon na do is prescribe you you're taking the the prednisone i'm also gon na give you a lidocaine swish and swallow and you can do that four times a day and so that will be able to help you so you can eat immediately after taking it and it can also help so that you can continue to take food and fluids prevent dehydration and any further weight loss

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: great

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay any questions for me

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't believe so at this time

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so i'll see you at your next visit

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: great thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're welcome and so now just

detail_list.summary: Follow-up for stage III non-small cell lung cancer.
The doctor asks about the patient's current symptoms.
The patient reports a minimal cough and a sore throat.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing fatigue.
The patient reports experiencing some fatigue.
The doctor asks if the patient has nausea or vomiting.
The patient denies nausea or vomiting.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing shortness of breath.
The patient denies shortness of breath and describes a dry cough.
The doctor asks if the cough is productive or dry.
The patient confirms the cough is dry.
The doctor asks for more information regarding the sore throat.
The patient reports the sore throat is persistent and fluctuates in severity.
The doctor asks if the patient feels like food is getting stuck when swallowing.
The patient denies dysphagia but reports decreased appetite.
The doctor asks about the patient's emotional state and support systems.
The patient reports feeling strong and supported by the nursing staff.
The doctor asks about family or friend support.
The patient has good family support and family members accompany her to chemotherapy.
The doctor confirms the patient is receiving Cisplatin and etoposide chemotherapy.
The patient confirms no nausea and only slight fatigue.
Physical exam shows normal vitals, no lymphadenopathy, regular heart rhythm, bilateral lung crackles, chest erythema, and no edema; chest x-ray shows mild radiation pneumonitis.
Lung cancer treatment plan.
Radiation pneumonitis treatment plan.
Prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days.
Painful swallowing treatment plan.
Lidocaine viscous solution 4 times daily.
The doctor asks if the patient has any questions.
The patient has no further questions.
The patient will return for the next scheduled follow-up visit.

case_description: follow-up for lung cancer; pneumonitis;painful swallowing
